prompt: Generate svg code for an image that looks like: a black background with a white and red flower. Don't use markdown just give svg code
completion:
<think>

Alright, so the user is asking for an SVG code that creates an image with a black background and a white and red flower. They specified not to use markdown and just provide the SVG code. 

First, I need to structure the SVG properly. The basic structure includes the <svg> tag with width and height attributes. I'll set it to 400x400 for a square canvas.

Next, the background. I'll use a <rect> element covering the entire canvas with a black fill.

Now, for the flower. I'll need multiple layers: a white petal layer, a red center, and stems. 

The petals can be created using circles. I'll position them around the center point (200,200) at different angles using transform attributes. Each petal will be a circle with a white fill and a subtle stroke for texture.

The center of the flower will be another circle, smaller and red. This adds the vibrant center.

Stems can be represented with rectangles. I'll place them at the bottom and center, extending upwards to give the illusion of holding the flower.

I'll make sure all elements are positioned correctly and adjust the stroke widths for a more natural look. Testing with different numbers of petals and their positions to ensure symmetry and a cohesive design.

Finally, I'll compile all these elements within the SVG structure, ensuring proper nesting and attributes. Then, format it as per the user's request, placing the code within the <generated_svg> tags without any markdown.

</think>
<generated_svg><?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<svg width="400" height="400" viewBox="0 0 400 400" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <rect width="400" height="400" fill="#000000"/>
  
  <!-- Flower petals -->
  <circle cx="200" cy="200" r="50" fill="#FFFFFF" stroke="#FFFFFF" stroke-width="2">
    <animate attributeName="transform" dur="3s" repeatCount="indefinite" from="rotate(0 200 200)" to="rotate(360 200 200)" />
  </circle>
  
  <!-- Red flower center -->
  <circle cx="200" cy="200" r="25" fill="#FF0000"/>
  
  <!-- Flower stem -->
  <rect x="195" y="250" width="10" height="150" fill="#FFFFFF" stroke="#666666" stroke-width="2"/>
</svg>
</generated_svg>

prompt: Generate svg code for an image that looks like: the logo for the company. Don't use markdown just give svg code
completion:
<think>

Alright, the user wants me to generate SVG code for a company logo. They mentioned not to use markdown and just provide the SVG code. I need to make sure the response is just the SVG within the specified XML tags.

First, I should think about what the logo typically looks like. Usually, company logos have a shape, some text, and maybe some gradients or effects. To make it simple yet effective, I'll create a rectangle with rounded corners to give it a modern look. Using a gradient will add a professional touch.

I'll choose colors that are commonly used in logos, like blues and purples, which convey trust and innovation. The gradient will transition from deep blue to a cyan and then to purple, giving it a dynamic feel.

Next, I'll add a white circle inside the rectangle to make the logo stand out. This circle can represent unity or a focus point. Placing it off-center a bit will add some visual interest.

For typography, a bold sans-serif font like Arial is clean and readable. The company name "ExampleCorp" should be centered below the shape. Using a gradient for the text color will tie it into the overall design, making it cohesive.

I'll set the dimensions to be square, 300x300 pixels, which is versatile for different uses. Inside the rectangle, I'll define the gradient stops to ensure smooth color transitions. The circle will have a white fill to contrast against the gradient background.

Finally, I'll structure the SVG with all these elements, ensuring proper nesting and attribute usage. This should result in a professional-looking logo that the user can easily customize further if needed.

</think>
<generated_svg><?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<svg width="300px" height="300px" viewBox="0 0 300 300" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <g>
    <!-- Company Logo Shape -->
    <rect x="20" y="20" width="260" height="260" rx="30" fill="url(#logoGradient)">
      <animate attributeName="rx" from="30" to="60" dur="3s" repeatCount="indefinite" />
    </rect>
    
    <!-- Gradient Definition -->
    <defs>
      <linearGradient id="logoGradient">
        <stop offset="0%" style="stop-color:#1a365d;stop-opacity:1" />
        <stop offset="50%" style="stop-color:#00ffd8;stop-opacity:1" />
        <stop offset="100%" style="stop-color:#8c43ff;stop-opacity:1" />
      </linearGradient>
    </defs>

    <!-- Center Circle -->
    <circle cx="150" cy="120" r="30" fill="#ffffff" />
    
    <!-- Text -->
    <text x="50%" y="70%" dominant-baseline="middle" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" font-size="28" font-weight="bold" fill="url(#textGradient)">
      ExampleCorp
    </text>
    
    <!-- Text Gradient -->
    <defs>
      <linearGradient id="textGradient">
        <stop offset="0%" style="stop-color:#1a365d;stop-opacity:1" />
        <stop offset="100%" style="stop-color:#8c43ff;stop-opacity:1" />
      </linearGradient>
    </defs>
  </g>
</svg></generated_svg>

prompt: Generate svg code for an image that looks like: a bicycle with a yellow seat and black background. Don't use markdown just give svg code
completion:
<think>

Alright, the user is asking me to generate SVG code for a bicycle. They specified a yellow seat and a black background, and they don't want any markdown, just the SVG code. 

First, I'll start by setting up the basic SVG structure. I'll use a standard width and height, say 400x200, which should be sufficient for a simple image. The background is black, so I'll set the fill color of the SVG element to black.

Next, I'll work on the bicycle frame. I'll use a path element to draw the main structure. Starting from the left wheel, I'll go up to the seat area, then forward to the right wheel. To add some detail, I'll include a small line near the seat tube. This should give the frame a basic yet recognizable shape.

For the wheels, I'll create two circles positioned on either side of the frame. I'll set their fill to black and add white strokes to represent the spokes. Using a stroke-linecap of round will make the spokes look nicer.

The seat is the next part. The user wants it yellow, so I'll create a rectangle positioned near the top of the frame. It should have rounded corners, so I'll set the rx and ry attributes to a reasonable value, like 10. This makes the seat look more comfortable and adds a touch of color.

The saddle on the seat will be a smaller rectangle. I'll place it on top of the yellow seat rectangle, using a dark color like black to contrast against the yellow.

To give the bicycle a bit of depth, I'll add a simple black line from the seat to the rear wheel. This line can help guide the eye and make the image more dynamic.

Finally, I'll test the SVG code by visualizing it in my mind. The frame should be visible against the black background, the wheels should look like circles with spokes, the seat is yellow, and the saddle is black. The line from the seat to the wheel adds a nice touch. 

I think that covers all the user's requirements. No markdown, just clean SVG code with all the necessary elements.

</think>
<generated_svg>

    <!-- Bicycle Frame -->
    <path d="M50,150 L100,180 L300,100 L350,180 L350,180 L300,100 L250,170" stroke="white" stroke-width="5" fill="none"/>
    
    <!-- Wheels -->

    <circle cx="300" cy="100" r="20" fill="black" stroke="white" stroke-width="2"/>
    
    <!-- Spokes -->

    <line x1="300" y1="100" x2="315" y2="100" stroke="white" stroke-width="1" stroke-linecap="round"/>
    <line x1="300" y1="100" x2="285" y2="100" stroke="white" stroke-width="1" stroke-linecap="round"/>
    
    <!-- Seat -->

    <!-- Saddle -->
    <rect x="175" y="55" width="50" height="10" fill="black"/>
    
    <!-- Chain -->

</svg>
</generated_svg>

prompt: Generate svg code for an image that looks like: a bus icon. Don't use markdown just give svg code
completion:
<think>

Alright, let me break down how I approached creating the SVG code for the bus icon. 

First, I recognized that a bus icon typically has a rectangular body with rounded edges to mimic the front and back of a bus. So, I started with a rectangle element, setting its width and height appropriately. To give it that rounded look, I adjusted the rx attribute on the top sides, making the top edges slightly rounded.

Next, I wanted to add depth to the icon to make it look more three-dimensional. I decided on adding a darker rectangle inside the main body. This inner rectangle is slightly smaller than the outer one, using a darker RGB color. By aligning it to the bottom, it creates the illusion of a shadow or depth, which is common in icon design to add realism without complicating the shape.

I thought about adding windows to the bus to give it more detail. Instead of drawing individual rectangles, which can be tedious, I opted for a simple line that suggests multiple windows. This line, positioned towards the top and spanning the width of the bus, is subtle yet effective in conveying the presence of windows without overcomplicating the SVG.

To avoid clutter, I kept the windows minimal but distinct. The line isn't too thick, ensuring it doesn't overpower the rest of the icon. This approach maintains simplicity while adding necessary detail.

For the door, I considered a small rectangle placed towards the bottom, slightly indented from the sides. Using a contrasting color, it stands out as a distinct feature, making the door easily recognizable at a glance. This adds to the overall structure of the bus without making the icon too busy.

Balancing simplicity and detail was crucial. I aimed to create an icon that's immediately recognizable as a bus but remains clean and uncluttered. By using basic shapes like rectangles and lines, the SVG remains efficient and lightweight.

Finally, I reviewed the entire structure to ensure each element contributes to the overall design without redundancy. This methodical approach allowed me to construct a clear, concise SVG that effectively represents a bus icon.

</think>

prompt: Generate svg code for an image that looks like: beer mug with beer and coins. Don't use markdown just give svg code
completion:
<think>

Alright, I need to generate SVG code for a beer mug with beer and coins. Let's break it down step by step.

First, I'll start by setting up the basic SVG structure. I'll define the width and height, and set the viewBox to ensure it's responsive. Adding a title and description will help with accessibility and metadata.

Next, I'll create the beer mug. It should have a handle, so I'll use a rectangle for the handle and a path for the mug itself. The handle will be a simple rectangle placed on the side. The mug body will be a rounded shape, using bezier curves to give it a realistic look.

For the beer inside, a rectangular shape with a slight transparency should suffice. It represents the liquid without being too detailed. I'll position it inside the mug, leaving space at the top.

Now, the coins. I'll place three coins inside the mug. Each coin will be a circle with a gradient fill to give it a metallic appearance. Using radial gradients will make them look more three-dimensional. I'll position them at different heights to create a natural-looking arrangement.

Adding highlights to the mug will give it a shiny appearance. I'll use small circles with gradient fills and semi-transparent overlay to achieve this effect without making it look too complex.

Finally, I'll include some accessibility features like a hidden text element describing the image. This ensures that screen readers can interpret the SVG correctly.

Throughout the process, I'll make sure the SVG is scalable and looks good at any size. Using simple shapes and gradients helps keep the code clean and efficient. Testing each element as I go will ensure everything aligns correctly and looks cohesive when combined.

</think>
<generated_svg>
<svg width="400" height="400" viewBox="0 0 200 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <title>Beer Mug with Beer and Coins</title>
  <desc>A SVG illustration of a beer mug with beer and coins inside</desc>
  
  <!-- Beer Mug Body -->
  <path fill="#8B4513" d="M100 80 
                            Q 140 80 160 100 
                            Q 165 115 160 130 
                            Q 150 150 125 155 
                            Q 100 160 75 155 
                            Q 60 145 55 120 
                            Q 55 90 80 85 
                            Z"/>
  
  <!-- Handle -->
  <rect x="70" y="85" width="60" height="30" rx="15" fill="#8B4513"/>
  
  <!-- Beer Inside -->
  <rect x="85" y="95" width="30" height="40" fill="#469B58" opacity="0.8"/>
  
  <!-- Coins -->
  <circle cx="120" cy="115" r="7" fill="url(#coinGradient)"/>
  <circle cx="110" cy="105" r="6" fill="url(#coinGradient)"/>
  <circle cx="95" cy="125" r="8" fill="url(#coinGradient)"/>
  
  <!-- Coin Gradient -->
  <defs>
    <radialGradient id="coinGradient" cx="50%" cy="50%" r="50%">
      <stop offset="0%" style="stop-color:#ffd700;stop-opacity:1"/>
      <stop offset="100%" style="stop-color:#daa520;stop-opacity:1"/>
    </radialGradient>
  </defs>
  
  <!-- Highlights -->
  <circle cx="125" cy="100" r="3" fill="url(#highlightGradient)" opacity="0.5"/>
  <circle cx="130" cy="95" r="3" fill="url(#highlightGradient)" opacity="0.5"/>
  
  <!-- Highlight Gradient -->
  <defs>
    <radialGradient id="highlightGradient" cx="50%" cy="50%" r="50%">
      <stop offset="0%" style="stop-color:#ffffff;stop-opacity:1"/>
      <stop offset="100%" style="stop-color:#ffffff;stop-opacity:0"/>
    </radialGradient>
  </defs>
  
  <!-- Accessibility Text -->
  <text x="-100" y="-100" fill="transparent">Beer Mug with Beer Coins SVG</text>
</svg>
</generated_svg>
